strengths: 17+ years of continuous software engineering experience across web, mobile, embedded, and infrastructure domains — meets the 15-year minimum
Founded GISCollective, an open-source geo-data platform actively used in production — demonstrates entrepreneurial builder instinct within a larger mission
Consistent knowledge-sharing through conference talks (FOSS4G 2019) and multi-day workshops — signals ability to inspire and educate technical communities
concerns: AI expertise is superficial and very recent: the only AI-related work is MCP tooling and agentic workflow infrastructure starting April 2025 — no demonstrated AI products, ML systems, or AI platform strategy at scale
No advanced degree in an AI-related field; the role strongly prefers Master's or PhD in AI/ML, and the candidate holds only a BS in Computer Science
No evidence of director-level IC scope: no strategic customer advisory, no executive-level influence, no cloud platform architecture ownership — the career arc plateaus at Staff/Senior Developer
response.justification: The role's core competency is demonstrated AI/ML expertise used to shape cloud and AI product strategy at a director IC level. The candidate's only AI-adjacent work is a single bullet from April 2025 describing MCP code generators and agentic workflow tooling — there is no evidence of building AI-powered products, designing ML systems, or advising enterprise customers on AI adoption, all of which are explicitly required responsibilities. The 17-year track record meets the experience floor, and founding GISCollective shows genuine builder entrepreneurship, but neither compensates for the critical gap in AI depth or the absence of strategic advisory scope. Cloud exposure is shallow (AWS CDK, Kubernetes as a deployment target) and does not approach the cloud platform architecture fluency the role requires. Score is derived from meeting the experience minimum and demonstrating entrepreneurial instinct, offset heavily by the absent AI competency and lack of director-level evidence.
